title: Simple oak bookends
subtitle: A pair of chamfered solid oak blocks for a bookshelf
excerpt: A pair of bookends cut from a single piece of seasoned oak: each block 12 cm tall, 10 cm deep, and 4 cm wide, with chamfered top edges and a Danish oil finish. The weight of solid oak does the work; no complicated joinery, no metalwork. A project that takes one afternoon and lasts for decades.
sourceNotes: Traditional British domestic treen; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. II for simple block bookend proportions.

content.text: Solid oak is heavy enough to anchor a row of books without any base plate or anti-slip device; a 12 by 10 by 4 cm block weighs approximately 600 grams, which is sufficient for a standard bookshelf row. The only hand tool work required is marking and chiselling the chamfered top edges.
Seasoned wood
oak is essential; green or partially seasoned oak will continue to move after shaping and may crack across the face grain.
The wood

A piece of kiln-dried oak: 25 cm long, 10 cm deep, 4 cm thick. Cut in half to produce the two bookend blocks. Quarter-sawn oak shows the medullary ray figure on the face; a striking silver fleck pattern that is characteristic of oak. Flat-sawn oak is plainer; both work for bookends. Ash is an alternative: similar density, more pronounced grain lines.
Tools

A bench chisel and carpenter's mallet for the chamfer cuts. A marking knife to score the chamfer boundary lines. A leather strop. Danish oil for finishing. A workbench or a clamp to hold the block during chiselling.
Method
Cut the
chamfer
lines with the marking knife before any chisel work. The marking knife scores a clear
stop cut
that prevents the chisel from tearing past the intended chamfer boundary.

Mark the chamfer lines on the top edges of both blocks: 8 mm in from the top edge on both the face and the side face. Score these lines with the marking knife; a single firm pass, 1 mm deep. The scored line is the boundary the chisel must not cross.

Clamp the block to the bench. Set the bench chisel at 45 degrees to the top edge, bevel facing down. Strike with the mallet in short controlled blows, working along the chamfer from one end to the other. Remove material in 2 mm passes; do not try to take the full chamfer in one pass. Oak's hardness makes it easy to overshoot the stop cut with a heavy blow.

Work the four top edges of each block; the two long edges and the two short ends at the top. The front face and the two side faces each receive a chamfer; the back face does not, as it will sit against the books.

Sand the chamfer faces through 120 and 180 grit. The flat faces of the block need only 180 grit; oak's tight grain does not require heavy sanding to produce a clean surface. Do not sand across the grain on the face; sand only with the grain to avoid scratches that show under the oil.

Apply cork sheet or felt to the base of each block with woodworking adhesive to protect the shelf surface. Cut the cork or felt slightly smaller than the base of the block so it does not show when the bookend is in use.
Finishing

Apply one coat of Danish oil to all surfaces except the cork base. Let it soak for 20 minutes and wipe off the excess. Danish oil sets in 24 hours. Oak darkens slightly with Danish oil, which brings out the grain and the ray figure. A second coat after 48 hours deepens the colour and increases surface protection.
Variations
Ash: lighter weight, more pronounced parallel grain lines. Use the same dimensions and method.
Larger format: increase to 15 cm tall and 12 cm deep for a heavier bookend that holds larger art books.
Chip-carved face: add a simple chip-carved decorative band on the front face (3 rows of 8 mm triangles) before oiling.
Care
Dust regularly. Re-oil with Danish oil when the surface looks dry or dull; once a year is usually sufficient for indoor use. Replace the cork base when it wears.

attrs.intro: Common problems with the oak bookends.

items.fix: Deepen the stop cut with a second knife pass before chiselling. Keep the chisel at 45 degrees or flatter; a steeper angle drives the edge toward the face rather than along it.
items.cause: Marking knife stop cut not deep enough, or chisel advancing at too steep an angle
items.symptom: Chisel tears past the chamfer line into the face

items.fix: After every 3 to 4 mallet strokes, check the chamfer face for flatness by laying the chisel flat against it. The tracks are high spots; direct the next pass there.
items.cause: Too many small strokes without levelling between passes
items.symptom: Chamfer surface shows chisel tracks rather than a clean flat

items.fix: Apply a second coat specifically to the end grain faces after the first coat has set. End grain always absorbs more oil and needs a second pass to even out the finish.
items.cause: End grain absorbing more oil than face grain
items.symptom: Oil finish is patchy; some areas absorb more than others

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
